feature: Rust Workspace Boundary Analysis in hex analyze
description: Extend hex analyze to detect and report Rust workspace layer counts and boundary violations. Fixes the broken design where 299 Rust files (87% of codebase) are invisible to the health score. Implements ADR-2603283000 P0-P5.

tasks.name: scan_rust_workspace_layers() — path-pattern layer detection
tasks.description: In hex-cli/src/commands/analyze.rs, add fn scan_rust_workspace_layers(root: &Path) -> Vec<(String, usize)>. Read root/Cargo.toml to find workspace.members (use toml crate already in Cargo.toml, or fall back to glob for */Cargo.toml). For each member crate, walk src/ and classify files by path pattern: domain/ → 'Domain', ports/ → 'Ports', adapters/primary/ or commands/ or routes/ → 'Primary Adapters', adapters/secondary/ or adapters/ → 'Secondary Adapters', orchestration/ or usecases/ → 'Use Cases', anything else → 'Infrastructure'. Return Vec of (layer_label, count) aggregated across all crates. Crates with no recognized dirs contribute to Infrastructure only. Unit tests: given a mock workspace dir structure, assert correct layer counts are returned. Done when: cargo check -p hex-cli passes, unit tests pass.

phases.name: Display Rust Layers in CLI Output

tasks.name: Render 'Rust workspace layers' section in hex analyze output
tasks.description: In analyze.rs run() function, after the existing 'Hex layers' block (TypeScript), call scan_rust_workspace_layers(root) when has_cargo_toml is true. Print a 'Rust workspace layers:' section with the same ✓/✗ indicator and file count format used for TypeScript layers. Infrastructure entries with 0 counted boundary-relevant files are shown as '- <crate> (infrastructure)'. Display order: Project structure → TypeScript hex layers (if has_src) → Rust workspace layers (if has_cargo_toml) → violations → score. Done when: running hex analyze . shows Rust layer counts for hex-nexus, hex-core, hex-cli.

tasks.name: scan_rust_boundary_violations() — use statement layer checker
tasks.description: In analyze.rs, add fn scan_rust_boundary_violations(root: &Path) -> Vec<String>. For each Rust source file (not in tests/ dir), read lines and collect use statements, skipping lines after '#[cfg(test)]'. Classify the file's own layer (same path-pattern logic as P0.1). Classify the imported path's layer using heuristics: use hex_core::domain → Domain, use hex_core::ports → Ports, use crate::adapters → SecondaryAdapter (relative), use hex_nexus::adapters from a domain file → violation. Apply rules from ADR-2603283000: (1) Domain must not import Adapters, (2) Secondary adapters must not import other secondary adapters (use crate::adapters::<other>), (3) Primary adapters must not import secondary adapters directly (only via ports). Return Vec<String> of human-readable violation messages with file:line. Integrate into the existing violations display block in run(). Done when: zero violations on clean hex-intf codebase (S09 spec passes), artificial violation in a test fixture is detected.

tasks.name: Include Rust file count in health score total_files denominator
tasks.description: In analyze.rs, the health score is computed from total_files (currently only TypeScript src/ files). Add rust_file_count = total count of .rs files across all workspace crate src/ dirs (including infrastructure crates). Update total_files += rust_file_count before score computation. The score formula itself does not change — only the denominator grows to reflect the real codebase size. Also add rust_violations_count to the violation penalty. Done when: hex analyze . reports total file count ≥ 400 (was 452 in last run) and score reflects Rust violations if any exist.

tasks.name: Add rust_layers and rust_violations to run_json() output
tasks.description: In analyze.rs run_json(), add rust_layers: Vec<{layer: String, file_count: usize}> and rust_violations: Vec<String> to the AnalysisResult struct (or the serde_json::json! object used for output). Call scan_rust_workspace_layers() and scan_rust_boundary_violations() the same as the human-readable path. Ensure --json output is machine-parseable and schema-compatible with any existing consumers (check hex-cli/src/commands/ for callers of run_json). Done when: hex analyze . --json includes rust_layers array in output.

tasks.name: Unit tests for scan_rust_workspace_layers
tasks.description: In analyze.rs #[cfg(test)] block, add tests using tempdir (already in dev-deps): (1) workspace with hex-core/src/domain/ and hex-nexus/src/adapters/ produces correct Domain and Secondary Adapter counts; (2) crate with no recognized dirs produces Infrastructure count; (3) empty workspace produces empty vec. Done when: cargo test -p hex-cli rust_workspace passes.

tasks.name: Unit tests for scan_rust_boundary_violations
tasks.description: In analyze.rs #[cfg(test)] block: (1) domain file with `use hex_nexus::adapters` produces violation; (2) secondary adapter file with `use crate::adapters::other` produces violation; (3) use inside #[cfg(test)] block is ignored; (4) clean file produces no violations. Done when: cargo test -p hex-cli rust_boundary passes.

tasks.name: Smoke test: hex analyze . produces zero Rust violations on hex-intf
tasks.description: Add an integration-style test (or doc-comment with example) that runs scan_rust_boundary_violations on the actual hex-intf root and asserts violations.is_empty(). This is the S09 spec. If violations are found, they are real bugs — fix them rather than ignoring them in the test. Done when: test passes on clean codebase.
